name: Blood Transfusion Ban
description: The "Blood Transfusion Ban" is primarily associated with the Jehovah's Witnesses religious denomination. Their interpretation of specific biblical verses leads them to refuse blood transfusions, viewing them as a violation of God's law against consuming blood. This stance is based on verses such as Genesis 9:4, Leviticus 17:10, and Acts 15:28-29. Proponents within the Jehovah's Witness community believe that accepting blood transfusions could jeopardize their salvation and relationship with God. They advocate for bloodless medicine and alternatives to transfusion. Mainstream medical science views blood transfusions as a life-saving medical procedure, crucial in treating severe anemia, trauma, surgery, and other conditions. The refusal of blood transfusions by Jehovah's Witnesses has led to ethical and legal conflicts, particularly when involving minors or incapacitated adults, where parental rights or individual autonomy clash with the perceived best interests of the patient from a medical perspective. Medical professionals generally support the right of competent adults to refuse medical treatment, even life-saving ones, but this right is not absolute and is subject to legal limitations in certain cases. Critics point out that the biblical interpretations used to justify the ban are open to alternative readings and that the benefits of blood transfusions in saving lives outweigh the perceived religious objections. Furthermore, the ban has been criticized for potentially causing unnecessary suffering and death, especially in situations where blood transfusion is the only viable treatment option.
subtitle: Religious objection to blood transfusions.

claims.claim: Blood transfusions are a violation of God's law.
claims.evidence: Interpretation of biblical verses such as Genesis 9:4, Leviticus 17:10, and Acts 15:28-29 forbidding the consumption of blood.
claims.criticism: Mainstream theology argues that these verses refer to the literal consumption of animal blood and do not apply to modern medical procedures like blood transfusions. Medical science emphasizes the life-saving benefits of blood transfusions.

claims.claim: Accepting blood transfusions jeopardizes one's salvation.
claims.evidence: Belief that obedience to God's law is essential for salvation and that violating this law through blood transfusion results in divine disapproval.
claims.criticism: This is a theological claim specific to Jehovah's Witnesses. Other Christian denominations do not share this view and consider blood transfusions a morally acceptable medical treatment. The concept of salvation varies across religious beliefs.

claims.claim: Bloodless medicine provides viable alternatives to blood transfusions.
claims.evidence: Development and use of techniques such as cell salvage, volume expanders, and erythropoietin to minimize or eliminate the need for blood transfusions.
claims.criticism: While bloodless medicine techniques are valuable, they are not always sufficient in cases of severe blood loss or specific medical conditions. Blood transfusions remain the most effective treatment in many situations.

keyFigures.name: Charles Taze Russell
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Bible Student movement (later Jehovah's Witnesses), whose teachings laid the groundwork for the blood transfusion ban.

keyFigures.name: Joseph Franklin Rutherford
keyFigures.role: Second president of the Watch Tower Society, who solidified the ban on blood transfusions.

keyFigures.name: Leo Twersky
keyFigures.role: Physician who advocates for bloodless medicine and assists Jehovah's Witnesses in finding alternatives to blood transfusions.

mythologyConnections: Ancient cultures' views on blood as life force
Ritualistic blood offerings in various religions

timeline.event: The Watch Tower Society officially prohibited blood transfusions for its members.
The Watch Tower Society published 'Blood, Medicine, and the Law of God', further solidifying their stance.
Legal and ethical challenges arise globally regarding the right to refuse blood transfusions, particularly in cases involving minors.

sources.author: Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society
sources.title: Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society
